title: Stanford AI deployment three-body failure
summary: A 95%-accurate pneumonia diagnostic AI was shut down six months after deployment. The model worked; the coordination with radiologist workflow and hospital operations did not. Optimizing one body (capability) while ignoring the others produced resistance, liability ambiguity, and reimbursement refusal.
source: Ch09 opening — "The technology worked. The coordination failed."

nodes.label: Integration Coordination
nodes.description: Missing third-body layer that would align all three

edges.description: Unilateral AI accuracy undermines radiologist trust
Capability without protocol creates liability ambiguity
Coordination layer would align workflow with AI
Coordination layer would align operations with AI

identifiability_note: Integration Coordination (n5) is a latent confounder on both the workflow and operations pathways; the marginal effect of AI capability on deployment outcome cannot be estimated without measuring the coordination layer.
